industries.name: Fundamental Nuclear & Atomic Research
uses: Named after Nicolaus Copernicus. Produced at GSI by bombarding lead-208 with zinc-70 ions. Copernicium is predicted to be gaseous or at most a very volatile liquid at room temperature due to extreme relativistic contraction of its 7s² electrons, weakening metallic bonding. One gas-phase experiment at PSI tentatively suggested Cn behaves more like a noble gas than mercury.
Longest-lived known isotope: 285Cn, t½ = 29 seconds. First synthesized: 1996, GSI Darmstadt (Hofmann group).

Research probes the limits of nuclear stability, tests relativistic quantum chemistry predictions in superheavy atoms, and searches for the theoretical island of stability predicted around Z=114, N=184.
All production requires specialized particle accelerators and isotope reactor facilities — this element cannot be made by any other means.

companies.name: GSI Helmholtz Centre
companies.use: Discovered copernicium in 1996 — the last element GSI discovered before JINR Dubna's hot fusion program took over superheavy element synthesis leadership. The SHIP separator detected individual Cn atoms by their characteristic alpha decay chain.

companies.name: Paul Scherrer Institute (PSI)
companies.use: Performed a preliminary gas-phase volatility study of copernicium, suggesting it may adsorb on gold surfaces at unexpectedly low temperatures — tentative evidence for noble-gas-like volatility predicted by relativistic theory. Results are controversial and require confirmation.

companies.name: RIKEN Nishina Center
companies.use: Conducted nuclear spectroscopy of copernicium isotopes using the GARIS separator, contributing decay chain data that independently confirmed GSI's discovery and characterized additional Cn isotopes with varying neutron numbers.
